sentence: Subjective:

HPI: The patient, a 45-year-old male with a history of diabetes type two and mild intermittent asthma, presents with right elbow pain that started three weeks ago after increasing his tennis activity.
The pain is described as sharp, stabbing, aching, and throbbing, with a severity of 8/10 when using the arm and 3/10 when resting.
The pain is localized to the outside part of the elbow and does not radiate to the arm or shoulder.
The patient has tried taking Tylenol and ibuprofen, but they do not provide significant relief.
Past medical history: The patient has a history of diabetes type two, mild intermittent asthma, and a surgical history of gallbladder removal 10 years ago.
He has a known allergy to penicillin.
He is currently taking metformin 500mg twice a day for his diabetes.
Review of systems: The patient reports that his asthma has been under control, with no recent asthma attacks.
He has not noticed any fever with the elbow pain.
Current medications: Metformin 500mg twice a day.
Objective:
Vital signs: Not mentioned.
Physical exam: The patient has moderate lateral epicondylar tenderness of the right elbow, with pain on passive range of motion.
There is no palpable joint effusion.
The patient has mild pain with resisted extension of the right wrist.
The heart exam is normal, with a grade 3/6 systolic ejection murmur.
The rest of the exam is unremarkable.
Test Results: The patient's right elbow x-ray shows no acute fracture or other bony abnormality.
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient has acute lateral epicondylitis of the right elbow, also known as tennis elbow, likely due to overuse from increased tennis activity.
Problem list: Acute lateral epicondylitis of the right elbow; diabetes type two; mild intermittent asthma.
Plan:
I prescribed ultram 50mg once a day for the elbow pain.
I want the patient to rest his right arm as much as possible, stop tennis for 4-6 weeks, and avoid repetitive stress in the right arm.
I also recommend icing the right elbow for 20 minutes, 3 times a day.
I will provide a brace for the right forearm to help with discomfort.
I will see the patient in follow-up in 4 weeks to reevaluate the elbow.
For the patient's diabetes type two, I increased the metformin to 1000mg twice a day and ordered a hemoglobin A1c level, CBC, and chem-12 blood test.
For the patient's asthma, I will keep him on flovent and albuterol as tolerated and will provide a refill for the albuterol.
Follow-up: The patient will return in 4 weeks for follow-up.
